elements.sent-2411-001: Our method consists of a Data Engine powered by 2D foundation models -- SAM \& Gemini -- that automatically annotates 3D assets from the web.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using the labeled data, \method trains a transformer-based point cloud model with a contrastive-based training recipe.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our method can segment diverse 3D objects with a rich set of part queries, such as the easel, as well as in-the-wild 3D reconstructions obtained via image-to-3D methods, such as the push car captured with an iPhone and the capybara with a hat generated by DALL-E, shown at the bottom right.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-004: We study open-world part segmentation in 3D: segmenting any part in any object based on any text query.
elements.sent-2411-005: Prior methods are limited in object categories or part vocabularies.
elements.sent-2411-006: Recent advances in AI have demonstrated effective open-world recognition capabilities in 2D.
elements.sent-2411-007: Inspired by this progress, we propose an open-world, direct-prediction model for 3D part segmentation that can be applied zero-shot to any object.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our approach, called \method, trains a general-category point embedding model on large-scale 3D assets from the internet without any human annotation.
elements.sent-2411-009: It combines a data engine, powered by foundation models for annotating data, with a contrastive training method.
elements.sent-2411-010: We achieve strong performance and generalization across multiple datasets, with up to a 3$\times$ improvement in mIoU over the next best method.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our model is 6$\times$ to over 300$\times$ faster than existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-012: To encourage research in general-category open-world 3D part segmentation, we also release a benchmark for general objects and parts.. Project website: \url{https://ziqi-ma.github.io/find3dsite/.}
elements.sent-2411-013: We study open-world part segmentation in 3D.
elements.sent-2411-014: By open-world, we mean that: 1) The input can be any object.
elements.sent-2411-015: Recent advancements in foundation models show that single models can achieve an impressive understanding of general concepts in language and 2D images.
elements.sent-2411-016: These results beg the question: how far are we from a foundation model that understands the 3D world? To answer this question, we first ask: can we build a model that can find any part of any object in 3D? Such a capability is critical to robotic and VR/AR applications where grounding objects and their parts in 3D is necessary for precise manipulation, interaction, and spatial awareness.
elements.sent-2411-017: We study the point cloud representation, which is the most readily available representation in such applications.
elements.sent-2411-018: Segmentation is a longstanding problem in computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the problem has generally been formulated in a closed world, and at the whole object level (\eg, ``car").
elements.sent-2411-020: Some efforts tackle perception below the object level (\eg, ``wheel of a car"), mainly driven by \shapenetpart~\cite{yi2016scalable , a closed-world benchmark of 16 object classes such as tables and chairs, with 6 or fewer predefined parts per object.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Recent vision and language foundation models have shown impressive zero-shot generalization capabilities in 2D image understanding ~\cite{radford2021learning, achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-022: These models are impressive as they operate in an open-world setting.
elements.sent-2411-023: Unfortunately, they do not apply to 3D representations.
elements.sent-2411-024: To build an effective open-world 3D model, 3D training data is needed for a diverse set of object types, shapes, and parts.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, it is not easy to acquire such data.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we develop a data engine for automatically labeling online 3D assets, and train an open-world 3D part segmentation model.
elements.sent-2411-027: The data engine is powered by 2D vision and language foundation models and annotates 3D objects with diverse part labels in an automated fashion.
elements.sent-2411-028: Using the data collected by the data engine, we train a transformer-based 3D model that takes in a point cloud and predicts queryable semantic features for every point.
elements.sent-2411-029: The features are in the latent embedding space of a CLIP-like~\cite{radford2021learning model, so that they can be queried with any free-form text by calculating pointwise cosine similarities with the query embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We devise a contrastive training objective to handle part hierarchy and ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-032: We build a zero-shot, part-level, open-world, direct-prediction model for 3D point cloud semantic segmentation that works on general object categories and part queries.
elements.sent-2411-033: We achieve 3$\times$ the mIoU and 6$\times$ to over 300$\times$ the inference speed compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-034: We develop a data engine that labels 3D object parts from large-scale internet data to train a general-category model without the need for human annotation.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our data engine creatively combines existing vision and language foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-036: We release a benchmark for evaluating open-world part-level semantic segmentation for general object categories with a diversity of objects and no pose constraints.

elements.sent-2411-040: Early efforts started with the \shapenetpart dataset~\cite{yi2016scalable (16 object classes, $\leq 6$ parts per object).}
elements.sent-2411-041: Due to the limited number of categories and shared orientations (\eg, chairs all facing right), state-of-the-art part-level models~\cite{qian2022pointnext, loizou2023cross cannot generalize well.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Our work tackles both the challenge of generalization and granularity -- our model is designed to be part-level, and can segment any object part in an open-world setting.
elements.sent-2411-043: With the progress of vision language models in 2D image understanding, some works directly assemble these models to obtain an ``aggregated" 3D understanding without training a 3D model.

elements.sent-2411-047: Because these models take features only from 2D, they lack 3D geometry information and suffer from inconsistency across views.
elements.sent-2411-048: Furthermore, these methods are slow because they perform many inferences and the aggregation logic at test time.

elements.sent-2411-052: At test time, these methods optimize the 3D representation with the 2D-sourced features attached.
elements.sent-2411-053: LERF~\cite{kerr2023lerf , Distilled Feature Field~\cite{shen2023f3rm , and Garfield~\cite{kim2024garfield are based on radiance fields.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Feature3DGS~\cite{zhou2024feature is based on Gaussian splatting.}
elements.sent-2411-055: These methods can provide good performance, but need to be optimized per scene (or per object), which can take several minutes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Besides being slow, the part-level capabilities have not been well-studied for such methods.
elements.sent-2411-057: Our method, being a feed-forward 3D model designed for part segmentation, provides much faster inference with better performance.. \mypar{Distillation methods.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Distillation methods train 3D models using 2D annotations.
elements.sent-2411-059: Generalization is a key limitation in prior works -- distillation is usually performed per dataset, even per category.
elements.sent-2411-060: OpenScene~\cite{peng2023openscene , a whole-object segmentation model for indoor scenes, is distilled per dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-061: For part segmentation, PartDistill~\cite{umam2024partdistill is distilled per category.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Such models cannot perform inference zero-shot on unseen object classes, which is critical in real-world use cases.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our approach can be considered a distillation method that tackles the challenge of zero-shot generalization.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{figs/method.pdf}} \caption{\method: an open-world part segmentation model. \method takes in a point cloud, voxelizes and serializes the points via space-filling curves into a sequence. The sequence is passed through a transformer architecture which performs block attention, shuffling, pooling and unpooling, returning a pointwise feature that is in the embedding space of a vision and language foundation model, denoted by $\mathcal{T}$. These features can be queried with any free-form text. \method is trained with a contrastive objective. For each (points, text embedding) label from the data engine, we use the averaged feature of these points as the predicted embedding, and pair it with the text embedding to form a positive pair in the contrastive loss.} \label{fig:method} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-064: We propose a method, \method, to locate any object part in 3D based on a free-form language description, such as ``the wheel of a car".
elements.sent-2411-065: As shown in \cref{fig:schematic (panel b), we design a model that takes in a point cloud and outputs a queryable semantic feature for every point.}
elements.sent-2411-066: This semantic feature is in the latent embedding space of a pre-trained CLIP-like~\cite{radford2021learning model, such as \siglip~\cite{zhai2023sigmoid .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: For any text query, we embed the query using the same model and calculate its cosine similarity with each point’s feature.
elements.sent-2411-068: This yields a pointwise similarity score that reflects the confidence of the part being located at that point.
elements.sent-2411-069: This score can be used to segment the object or localize specific parts.
elements.sent-2411-070: Formally, given a point cloud $C = \{\mathbf{p_i , ...{\mathbf{p_n }\}$ with color and normals, for any point $\mathbf{p_i =(x,y,z, n_x, n_y, n_z, r, g, b) \in \mathbb{R ^9$, we want to find a semantic feature $f_i \in \mathbb{R ^d$ which belongs in the same latent embedding space as a CLIP-like model, \eg, \siglip.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: These semantic features attached to the point cloud $C$ allow us to query the point cloud in 3D space in an open-world fashion -- for any text $s$, we can get its \siglip embedding $\mathcal{T (s)$ and compute its cosine similarity with $f_i$, $cos(\mathcal{T (s), f_i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: To obtain such a model, \method consists of a data engine and a contrastive training recipe.
elements.sent-2411-073: The data engine automatically annotates online 3D assets, leveraging 2D foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-074: These annotations are used to train a Transformer-based~\cite{vaswani2017attention model for 3D point clouds.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We use a contrastive learning objective to overcome the challenge of label ambiguity (e.g. one point associated with ``snowball" and ``body" from different views).
elements.sent-2411-076: Our data engine and contrastive training recipe enable scalable training on diverse objects and part labels.
elements.sent-2411-077: Obtaining large-scale 3D annotations for generic object categories with human-in-the-loop pipelines is onerous.
elements.sent-2411-078: To this end, we develop a scalable data engine that leverages annotations from 2D foundation models and geometrically unprojects them to 3D.
elements.sent-2411-079: As illustrated in ~\cref{fig:dataengine , Our data engine leverages SAM~\cite{kirillov2023segment and Gemini~\cite{reid2024gemini to annotate 3D assets from Objaverse~\cite{deitke2023objaverse .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Since Objaverse assets do not have a fixed orientation, and Gemini provides higher-quality labels to objects seen in familiar orientations, we first prompt Gemini to select the best orientation based on 10 renderings (from different camera angles) of an object in each orientation.
elements.sent-2411-081: For the chosen orientation, we pass all renderings to SAM with grid point prompts.
elements.sent-2411-082: We discard masks that are too small (less than 350 pixels out of a 500x500 image), too large (greater than 20\% of all pixels), or with low confidence from SAM.
elements.sent-2411-083: We overlay each mask on the original image and ask Gemini to name the shaded part.
elements.sent-2411-084: Prompts are detailed in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-085: Masks from the same camera view with the same label are merged.
elements.sent-2411-086: This process generates labeled (mask, text) pairs.
elements.sent-2411-087: We map each mask to a set of points in the point cloud based on projection geometry.
elements.sent-2411-088: To make the point features queryable by language, we align point features to the language embedding space of a pretrained model, such as \siglip.
elements.sent-2411-089: We embed the label texts and use the text features as supervision.
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus, our label (mask, text) pairs become (points, text embedding) pairs.
elements.sent-2411-091: The data engine processes 36044 high-quality objects of Objaverse under LVIS categories selected by ~\cite{long2024wonder3d, wonder3drepo .}
elements.sent-2411-092: After filtering out objects with insufficient labels, we obtain 30K annotated objects from 761 categories with 1.5 million part annotations.
elements.sent-2411-093: Each part is annotated differently from different views, denoting various aspects of part, such as location (\eg, ``bottom"), material (\eg, ``snowball"), and function (\eg, ``body").
elements.sent-2411-094: Labels also have different levels of granularity.
elements.sent-2411-095: For example, in \cref{fig:dataengine , one granularity is individual snowballs, and another granularity is the whole snowman.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The diversity of our labels helps the model handle the inherent ambiguity in segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-097: As shown in \cref{fig:method , \method takes in a point cloud and returns a pointwise semantic feature that can be queried with any free-form text via cosine similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-098: For any number of user-specified text queries, \method first embeds them via the \siglip text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-099: To produce 3D segmentations, \method assigns each point to the text query with the highest cosine similarity, and assigns ``no label" if all queries yield negative similarity scores.
elements.sent-2411-100: We build upon the PointTransformer3 (PT3) model~\cite{wu2024point .}
elements.sent-2411-102: First, the point clouds are converted into sequences.
elements.sent-2411-103: To this end, the normalized point clouds are sampled with a voxel size of 0.02, so that there is at most one point per voxel.
elements.sent-2411-104: The voxels can be serialized in different methods, including Z-Ordering, Trans-Z-Ordering, Hilbert, and Trans-Hilbert \cite{morton1966computer, hilbert1935stetige, wu2024point .}
elements.sent-2411-105: These different serialization schemes are used in different layers in our architecture, following PT3.
elements.sent-2411-106: The serialized point cloud forms a sequence of length $N$, where $N$ is the number of points.
elements.sent-2411-107: Each point carries its XYZ, RGB, and normal information.
elements.sent-2411-108: These values are embedded with a linear layer into point embedding, and a conditional positional encoding~\cite{chu2021conditional is added to the embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The embedded sequence is then passed to the transformer, which comprises an encoder and a decoder.
elements.sent-2411-110: Since the point sequence can be arbitrarily long and the quadratic complexity of attention can grow prohibitively expensive, we chunk the sequence into 1024-size blocks as in the PT3 architecture.
elements.sent-2411-111: Attention is only performed within each block.
elements.sent-2411-112: To achieve the large receptive field of transformers, shuffling is applied per layer.
elements.sent-2411-113: Each shuffling operation maps the current serialization scheme to the (different) serialization scheme used by the next layer.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the encoder, pooling is applied per layer.
elements.sent-2411-115: Although pooling is dependent on the serialization scheme, the goal is to pool points that are neighbors in the 3D space, since all our serialization schemes preserve locality (to the extent possible).
elements.sent-2411-116: As a result, the sequence becomes shorter, and the receptive field becomes larger, even though block attention has a fixed size.
elements.sent-2411-117: In the decoder, this process is reversed -- unpooling happens by back-tracing to pooled points in the encoding stage.
elements.sent-2411-118: Block attention happens similarly to the encoder, while shuffling is also back-traced.
elements.sent-2411-119: Eventually, we recover a sequence of the same length as the input (i.e., number of points), but of a different latent dimension.
elements.sent-2411-120: Since we want to align the point feature into the latent embedding space of \siglip, we append a lightweight 4-layer MLP to the last layer of the transformer.
elements.sent-2411-121: This returns a 768-dimension feature per point.
elements.sent-2411-123: During training, the contrastive loss is only applied to points that are kept during voxel sampling.
elements.sent-2411-124: At inference time, the model only predicts features for the points that are kept during voxel sampling.
elements.sent-2411-125: For the points that are dropped, we assign the feature of the nearest neighbor which is kept during voxel sampling.
elements.sent-2411-126: The data engine provides diverse part labels.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, it is difficult to define a direct pointwise loss because: 1) The same point can have multiple labels that denote various aspects of a part such as location, material, and function.
elements.sent-2411-128: Some labels may also be incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-129: Due to these challenges, we resort to a contrastive loss that does not rely on per-point labeling.
elements.sent-2411-130: As illustrated on the right side of \cref{fig:method , we define the pairing as follows: for each label, the ground truth is the \siglip embedding of the text.}
elements.sent-2411-131: The predicted value is taken as the average feature of all points belonging to the label.
elements.sent-2411-132: This pooling can also be regarded as a way to ``denoise" the labels -- while an individual point might be affected by conflicting or incorrect labels, it is unlikely that all points are subjected to the same conflicting or incorrect labels.
elements.sent-2411-133: Our data engine provides (points, text embedding) labels, which we denote as $(C_i, \mathcal{T (\text{label _i))$ where $C_i$ is a subset of the point cloud that this label applies to, and $\mathcal{T (\text{label _i)$ is the label embedding.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: We denote the pooled feature from the labeled points as $f(C_i)$, where $f$ is our model.
elements.sent-2411-135: We define the contrastive loss as follows: \begin{equation}l_i=-\log \frac{\exp(f(C_{i}) \cdot \mathcal{T}(\text{label}_i))}{\sum_{j=1}^ {\vert\mathcal{B}\vert} \exp(f(C_{i}) \cdot \mathcal{T}(\text{label}_j))}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{B}$ is the indices of all labels of all objects in a batch.
elements.sent-2411-136: Because each label is unprojected from a single view, they only include points visible from that view and cover a part only partially.
elements.sent-2411-137: Although this illustration only shows one object, during training we include $64$ objects in the batch, corresponding to $\sim3000$ positive pairs per batch.
elements.sent-2411-138: To achieve generalization, in addition to training on diverse data provided by the data engine, we also apply data augmentations, including full random rotation (implemented as sequential random rotation along all three axes), scaling, flipping, jittering, chromatic auto contrast, chromatic translation, and chromatic jitter.
elements.sent-2411-139: These augmentations help avoid over-reliance on object poses and color, and nudges the model to take up 3D geometric cues.
elements.sent-2411-140: We perform a 90:10 train-validation split on the 27552 objects provided by the data engine, and train with the Adam optimizer~\cite{Kingma2014AdamAM with a cosine annealing learning rate schedule, starting at 0.0003 and ending at 0.00005 over 80 epochs.}

elements.figure-2411-015: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{figs/method.pdf}} \caption{\method: an open-world part segmentation model. \method takes in a point cloud, voxelizes and serializes the points via space-filling curves into a sequence.
